segments.text: Dormant viruses are waking up in space.
This is a big deal if we're sending more
people up there. It all started because
something weird happened on the Apollo
missions. Within a week of landing back
on Earth, over half of the astronauts
were sick with colds and other
infections. It turns out our immune
systems really need gravity. Within
minutes of launching into space, your
whole body starts to change. Gravity
that normally pulls fluid downward
suddenly disappears. And without that
pressure, fluids can't move cells around
in your body as easily. So, your immune
cells go from moving along this
superighway to just sort of lazily
bouncing along. If we look closer, it's
possible that even fluid inside your
immune cells need gravity to help them
send messages when they find intruders.
But the creepiest thing is that
something about gravity also keeps
dormant viruses locked up. Which is why
in space, viruses like chickenpox or
Epstein bar can suddenly wake up. All of
this means that we have to monitor the
health of astronauts very closely. Which
is why every day before astronauts on
the ISS do anything else, they take a
saliva sample to test for all sorts of
things from viruses to stress hormones.
And they'll be doing this on the Artemis
missions, too. If you've got to see more
optimistic science and tech stories,
subscribe.
